m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
 class ClassA {}
 class ClassB extends ClassA {}
 class ClassC extends ClassA {}
 and
 ClassA p0 = new ClassA();
 ClassB p1 = new ClassB();
 ClassC p2 = new ClassC();
 ClassA p3 = new ClassB();
 ClassA p4 = new ClassC();
Which is invalid?
m_Item1.Explanation:
A. p0 = p1;
p0 is of type ClassA and p1 is of type ClassB.
ClassB extends ClassA, so this assignment is valid (upcasting).
B. p1 = p2;
p1 is of type ClassB and p2 is of type ClassC.
Both ClassB and ClassC extend ClassA, but they are not directly related to each other (no inheritance relationship).
This assignment is invalid because ClassB and ClassC are not in an inheritance hierarchy.
C. p1 = (ClassB)p3;
p1 is of type ClassB and p3 is of type ClassA, referencing a ClassB object.
p3 is referencing a ClassB object, so this downcast is valid.
D. p2 = (ClassC)p4;
p2 is of type ClassC and p4 is of type ClassA, referencing a ClassC object.
p4 is referencing a ClassC object, so this downcast is valid.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
The following Syntax is used for?

class Subclass-name extends Superclass-name 
{ 
//methods and fields 
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Using extends keywork subclass can access the properties of super class.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following is true about inheritance in Java.
1) In Java all classes inherit from the Object class directly or indirectly. The Object class is root of all classes.
2) Multiple inheritance is not allowed in Java.
3) Unlike C++, there is nothing like type of inheritance in Java where we can specify whether the inheritance is protected, public or private.
m_Item1.Explanation:
•Inheritance is also known IS-A relationship.
•It provides child class the ability to inherit non-private members of parent class.
•In java, inheritance is achieved via extends keyword.
•From Java 8 onward, you can use interfaces with default methods to achieve multiple inheritance.
•Member fields are accessed from reference type class.
•Member methods are accessed from actual instance types

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is not correct?

Options.OptionText: You can extend an interface from another interface
You can define a variable inside an Interface
You can implement multiple interfaces for one class

m_Item1.Explanation: Interfaces can declare only final varibales

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
class Programmer
{
Programmer debug()
{
    return this;
}
}
class SCJP extends Programmer
{
         // insert code here
}
Which method inserted becomes example of method overriding?
m_Item1.Explanation: When method is overridden in the sublcass, its return type can be changed only to subtype. This is called covarinat return type. In the above code, subclass overridden method can return subclass of Programmer i.e. SCJP.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the result of compiling and running the following code?
class Base {
private Base() {
System.out.println("Base");
}
}
class Test extends Base {
public Test() {
System.out.println("Derived");
}
public static void main(String[] args) {
new Test();
}
}
m_Item1.Explanation: From the constructor of class Test, it needs default constructor of Base class which is visible as no other explicit constructor is called. In this case default constructor of Base class is decared as private. So it will generate compilation error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true about the super keyword?

Options.OptionText: It is used to call the constructor of the superclass.
It is used to access members of the superclass that are hidden by members of the subclass.
It can be used to call any method of the superclass, even private methods.

m_Item1.Explanation: super keyword can be used for calling super class constructors or to access members of super class. But private members are never accessible in subclass using super even.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the code below:
class A {
public void print() {
System.out.println("hi");
}
}
class B extends A {
public void print() {  
System.out.println("bye");
}
}
B b = new A();
b.print();

Options.OptionText: The code does not compile, the compiler returns a type error

m_Item1.Explanation: E

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program ?
class A {
public  int i;
protected int j;
}
class B extends A {
int j;
void display()
{
super.j=3;
System.out.println(i +" "+ j);
}
}
class Output {
public static void main(String args[])
{
B obj =new B();
obj.i=1;
obj.j=2;
obj.display();
}
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Value of i and j has been assigned as 1 and 2 by main method. display() method modifies value of j from class A as it makes use of super keyword. so display() method displays i and j as "1 2".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which is the first line that will cause compilation to fail in the following program?
class MyClass {
public static void main(String[] args) {
MyClass a;
MySubclass b;
a = new MyClass();       //(1)
b = new MySubclass();  //(2)
a = b;               //(3)
b = a;               //(4)
a = new MySubclass();  //(5)
}
}
class MySubclass extends MyClass
{
}
m_Item1.Explanation: E

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Determine output of the following code. 
interface A { }
class C { }
class D extends C { }
class B extends D implements A { }

public class Test extends Thread{
     public static void main(String[] args){
         B b = new B();
         if (b instanceof A)
             System.out.println("b is an instance of A");
         if (b instanceof C)
             System.out.println("b is an instance of C");
     }
}

Options.OptionText:
b is an instance of A
b is an instance of C

m_Item1.Explanation: Class B is derived class of Class C and Class D. So instance of B can access the properties of  all above base classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following does not belong: If a class inherits from some other class, it should

Options.OptionText: Make use of the parent class’s capabilities
Over-ride or add the minimum to accomplish the derived class’ purpose
Over-ride all the methods of its parent class
Make sure the result “IS-A-KIND-OF” its base class

m_Item1.Explanation: Inherits from other class means not all the methods are overridden. If you require then override not all are overriden. Its not compulsary.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
class San {
San() throws IOException
{
}
}
class Foundry extends San {
Foundry()
{
}
public static void main(String[]args)
{
}
}

Options.OptionText: unreported exception java.io.lOException in default constructor

m_Item1.Explanation: When the super class default constructor throws exception, it should be declared in the throws clause by subclass default constructor.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
class Uber {
static int y = 2;
Uber(int x) {
this();
y = y * 2;
}
Uber() {
y++;
}
}
class Minor extends  Uber {
Minor() {
super(y);
y = y + 3;
}
public static void main(String  [] args) {
new Minor();
System.out.println(y);
}
}
What is the result?
m_Item1.Explanation: Minor() constructor calls constructor Uber(int) which calls Uber(). In Uber() value of y becomes 3, the in Uber(int) constructor value of y becomes 6 and then in Minor() constructor value of y vecomes 9.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
class Dog {
public void bark() {
System.out.println("woof")'
}
}
class  Hound   extends  Dog {
public  void sniff() {
System.out.println("sniff");
}
public  void  bark() {
System.out.println("howl");
}
}
public class DogShow {
public static void main(String[]  args) {
new DogShow().go();
}
void go() {
new Hound().bark();
((Dog)new Hound()).bark();
((Dog)new Hound()).sniff();
}
}
what's  the result?

Options.OptionText: howl howl followed by an exception

m_Item1.Explanation: Dog class does not have sniff() method. Type casting Hound object to Dog and calling method sniff() gives compilation error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following are not access  specifiers?
m_Item1.Explanation: static is not access specifier.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Assume that class A extends class B, which extends class C. Also all the three classes implement the method test(). How can a method in a class A invoke the test() method defined in class C (without creating a new instance of class C). Select the one correct answer.
m_Item1.Explanation: super.test() will invoke super class's test method.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output for the below code?
public class A {
private void printName() {
System.out.println("Value-A");
}
}
public class B extends A {
public void printName() {
System.out.println("Name-B");
}
}
public class Test {
public static void main (String[] args)
{
B b = new B();
b.printName();
}
}

Options.OptionText: Compilation fails - private methods can't be overriden

m_Item1.Explanation: printName() method from class B gets called which prints "Name-B".

m_Item1.QuestionText: Suppose a class has public visibility.  In this class we define a protected  method.  Which of the Following  statement  is correct?

Options.OptionText: This method is accessible from inside the class itself and from inside all subclasses residing in any package.
In a class, you can not declare methods with a lower visibility than the visibility of the class in which it is defined.
From within protected methods you do not have access to public methods.
This method is accessible from within the class itself and from within all classes defined in the same package as the class itself.

m_Item1.Explanation: Protected members are accessible from the class and all subclasses in any package. Protected refers to inheritance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true?

Options.OptionText: Interfaces must be used when creating a has-a relationship
Instance variables can be used when creating a is-a relationship.

m_Item1.Explanation: Inheritance represents is-a relationship.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these class is superclass of all other classes ?
m_Item1.Explanation: class Object is default super class for all java classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the Output? 
class Animal {
String name = "animal";
String makeNoise() {
return "generic noise";
}
}
class Dog extends Animal {
String name = "dog";
String makeNoise() { 
return "bark";
}
}
public class Test  {
public static void main(String[] args){ 
Animal an = new Dog();
System.out.println(an.name+" "+an.makeNoise());
}
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Variable binding is static binding. So name gets displayed from Animal(type of reference) whereas method binding is dynamic binding so it gets called from type of instance ie. Dog

m_Item1.QuestionText:
class Atom {
Atom() {
System.out.print("atom ");
}
}
class Rock extends Atom {
Rock(String type) {
System.out.print(type);
}
}
public class Mountain extends Rock {
Mountain() {
super("granite ");
new Rock("granite ");
}
public static void main(String[] a) {
new Mountain();
}
}
What is the result?
m_Item1.Explanation: When Object of subclass is created then super class's construtcor is called. If no super() call is available in sub class constructor, it automatically calls super class default constructor.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the result?
11. public class Person {
12.          String name = "No name";
13.          public Person(String nm) { name = nm; }
14. }
15.
16. public class Employee extends Person {
17.          String empID = "0000";
18.          public Employee(String id) { empID = id; }
19. }
20.
21. public class EmployeeTest {
22.          public static void main(String[] args){
23.                 Employee e = new Employee("4321");
24.                 System.out.println(e.empID);
25.         }
26. }

Options.OptionText: An exception is thrown at runtime.
Compilation fails because of an error in line 18

m_Item1.Explanation: In subclass constructor, super class constructor should be called implicitly or explicitly. If no explicit call is given, it calls super class default constructor which is not provided in super class. So it leads to compilation error.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
class SuperA  {  String s1="SuperA"; }
class SuperB  { String s1="SuperB"; }
class A extends SuperA{
String s1="A";
class B extends SuperB
{  // 1
String s1="B";
void m1()
{
System.out.print(this.s1 + ",");   // 2
System.out.print(super.s1 + ",");  // 3
System.out.print(A.this.s1 + ","); // 4
System.out.print(A.super.s1);    // 5
        }
        }
public static void main (String[] args)
{
new A().new B().m1();  // 6
}
}
What is the result of attempting to compile and run the program?
m_Item1.Explanation: this.s1 is B, super.s1 is SuperB, A.this.s1 is A and A.super.s1 is SuperA

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the result of compiling and running the Second class? Consider the following example:
class First
{
public First (String s)
{
System.out.println(s);
}
}
public class Second extends First
{
public static void main(String args [])
{ 
new Second();
}
}

Options.OptionText: An instance of the class First is generated
An exception is raised at runtime stating that there is no null parameter constructor in class First.
The class second will not compile as there is no default constructor in class First

m_Item1.Explanation: At a time of creating object of subclass, super class default constructor will be invoked implicity if no constructor is explicitly called.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given these class definitions: 
class Superclass { } 
class Subclass1 extends Superclass { }
and these objects: 
Superclass a = new Superclass(); 
Subclass1 b = new Subclass1(); 
which of the following explains the result of the statement: 
a = b;

Options.OptionText: Legal at compile time but possibly illegal at runtime
Definitely legal at compile time and runtime

m_Item1.Explanation: Superclass reference can refer to subclass instance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if a subclass defines a method with the same signature as in the superclass?
m_Item1.Explanation: When a subclass defines a method with the same signature, it overrides the superclass method.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is true about constructors in inheritance?
m_Item1.Explanation: Constructors are not inherited in Java, but a subclass can call them using super().

m_Item1.QuestionText:
class A { 
    int x = 10; 
}
class B extends A { 
   int x = 20; 
   void print() { 
          System.out.println(super.x);
   } 
}
m_Item1.Explanation: super.x refers to the x variable in the superclass A, which is 10.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why does Java not support multiple inheritance with classes?
m_Item1.Explanation: Java avoids multiple inheritance with classes to prevent ambiguity, especially in cases where two superclasses have methods with the same signature (Diamond Problem).

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if super() is not the first statement in a subclass constructor?

Options.OptionText: The superclass constructor is not called
The code compiles but throws a runtime exception

m_Item1.Explanation: In Java, if you use super() to call a superclass constructor, it must be the first statement in the subclass constructor. If it's placed elsewhere, the compiler throws an error: "Constructor call must be the first statement in a constructor."

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of inheritance in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: Inheritance allows a new class to use properties and methods of an existing class, promoting code reusability.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following about java inheritance is true?

Options.OptionText: A subclass can be a superclass of another subclass.
A class can inherit multiple classes directly.
Private methods are accessible in the subclass.
Constructors are inherited from the superclass.

m_Item1.Explanation: A subclass can extend a superclass that is also the parent of another class, forming a hierarchy.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What keyword is used to restrict class inheritance in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'final' keyword is used to prevent a class from being extended.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does Java handle multiple inheritance?

Options.OptionText: Java allows multiple inheritance through classes.
Java does not support multiple inheritance at all.
Java allows multiple inheritance through interfaces.
Java allows multiple inheritance using inheritance trees.

m_Item1.Explanation: Java supports multiple inheritance through interfaces but does not allow classes to extend multiple other classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these is a valid type of inheritance in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: In hierarchical inheritance, multiple subclasses inherit from a single superclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which concept allows new methods to override existing ones in inheritance?
m_Item1.Explanation: Polymorphism allows methods to be overridden, providing different implementations for a method declared in a superclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is indicated by the 'super' keyword in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'super' keyword is used within a subclass to refer to a method or variable in its immediate superclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is required for a class to be inherited?

Options.OptionText: It must be declared as static.
It must not be declared final.
It must have public methods only.

m_Item1.Explanation: A 'final' class cannot be extended; thus, it cannot be inherited.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which is not related to inheritance in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'import' keyword is used for including external classes and packages, not related to inheritance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In inheritance, what does the subclass inherit from the superclass?
m_Item1.Explanation: The subclass inherits all public and protected members (methods and variables) from its superclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How is method overriding related to inheritance?

Options.OptionText: It provides specific implementation to inherited methods.

m_Item1.Explanation: Method overriding allows a subclass to provide a specific implementation for a method already defined in its superclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used for defining inheritance in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'extends' keyword is used when a class wants to inherit properties and methods from another class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the 'super()' call do in a subclass constructor?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Java, 'super()' is used in a subclass constructor to call its closest superclass constructor.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following inheritance relationships is legal in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: Class-to-class inheritance is possible using the 'extends' keyword in Java.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of inheritance, abstract classes are:
m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract classes provide a base for subclasses to implement its abstract methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which inheritance level is present when a class implements an interface?
m_Item1.Explanation: When a class implements an interface, it provides a concrete implementation for the interface's methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In what scenario will the constructor of a superclass be called automatically?

Options.OptionText: When a superclass method is invoked
When super() is called outside a constructor

m_Item1.Explanation: When a subclass object is created, its superclass constructor is automatically called before executing subclass constructor code.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What scenario is NOT an example of inheritance?

Options.OptionText: A superclass with abstract methods being extended
A child class implementing interface methods

m_Item1.Explanation: Declaring an interface is not an example of inheritance; it's creating a contract for classes to implement.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In order to check “is-a” relationship, we use following operator
m_Item1.Explanation: Write explaination here..

m_Item1.Explanation: Write explaination here..

m_Item1.QuestionText: Super must be on first line if we want to invoke base class constructor.
m_Item1.Explanation: Write explaination here..

m_Item1.QuestionText:
what is the result of following code ?
class base {
    int i; 
    base() {
      add(1);
    }
   void add(int v) {
      i+=v;
    }
    void print() {
      System.out.println(i);
   } 
}
class sub extends base {
   sub() {
      System.out.println(“in sub def const”);
      super.add(2);
    }
    void add(int v) {
       i+=v*2;
    }
}
public class test11 {
   public static void main(String args[]) {
        base b;
        b=new sub();
        b.print();
   }
}

Options.OptionText: Error: super.add(2) has to be on first line of constructor

m_Item1.Explanation: When new sub() gets executed, it first calls base() default constructor which assigns value 2 to i varibale. Further in subclass onstructor, it first prints the message "in sub def const" and then calls super.add(2) which assigns value 4 to i.

m_Item1.QuestionText: specify which of the following is true ?

Options.OptionText: protected members can not be accessed directly in the same package.
Protected member can be accessed with super class reference in different package.
Private member can be accessed by subclass using super keyword.

m_Item1.Explanation: Write explaination here..

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of following code.
class a {
    static {
            System.out.println(" static a");
        }
     }
    class b extends a {
    static {
            System.out.println(" static b");
        }
    }
   class c extends b {
        static {
            System.out.println(" static c");
        }
    }
    public class myclass {
        static {
            System.out.println(" static myclass");
    }    
    public static void main(String args[])  {
        new c();
        System.out.println("in main");
        }
}

Options.OptionText: in main, static a,static b,static c, static myclass
static myclass, static a,static b,static c, in main
static myclass, in main ,static a,static b,static c
static a,static b,static c, static myclass, in main ,

m_Item1.Explanation:
1. static myclass block executes.
  2. new c() causes the class c to be loaded, which triggers the loading of its superclasses.
  3. static a block executes (because a is the superclass of b and c).
  4. static b block executes (because b is the superclass of c).
  5. static c block executes.
  6. System.out.println("in main"); executes.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will happen to the following code ?
class Base {
    public final void disp ()  {
        System.out.println (“in disp”);
    }
}
public class Sub extends Base {
     public static void main (String argv [] )  {
            Base b = new Base() ;
            b.disp () ;
    }
}

Options.OptionText: compiler error “final method must be inside final class”
compiler error “a class having final method can not be inherited”

m_Item1.Explanation: When super class has any method declared as final, it can not be overridden by subclass. In the above case disp() method is not overridden in the subclass.

what will be the output ?

Options.OptionText: It leads to confusion for a Java program
The programmer can achieve multiple inheritance by using interface
The programmer can achieve multiple inheritance by repeatedly using single inheritance

m_Item1.QuestionText:
what is the output?
    class base { }
    class sub1 extends base  {   }
    class sub2 extends sub1 {    }
    class sub3 extends sub2 {    }
    public class test12 {
    public static void main(String args[])     {
        sub1 s=new sub2();
        base b=s;
        if(b instanceof base)      {
            System.out.println("base");
        }
        if(b instanceof sub1)         {
            System.out.println("sub1");
        }
        if(b instanceof sub2)         {
            System.out.println("sub2");
        }
        if(b instanceof sub3)         {
            System.out.println("sub3");
        }
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Object s is of type sub2 which has super classes as base and sub1. So instanceof operation will be true for base, sub1 and sub2 but not for sub3

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the following code,what can be said about the statement s=(sub)b ?
class base  {  }
class sub extends base {  }
public class test12  {
        public static void main(String args[])     {
                base b=new base();
                sub s=new sub();
                s=(sub)b;
    }
}

Options.OptionText: legal at compile time but illegal at runtime
legal at compile and runtime ,but (sub) cast is not needed
legal at compile and runtime ,but (sub) cast is strictly needed.

m_Item1.Explanation: In summary, s = (sub) b; attempts to cast a base reference b to a sub reference s, which is a downcasting operation. The success of this cast depends on whether b actually refers to an object of type sub or a subclass thereof.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Will following code work?
Class MyClass extends String
{}
m_Item1.Explanation:
No, the code will not work. In Java, the String class is final, which means it cannot be extended. The final keyword prevents a class from being subclassed.

Here is the reason why:

  • Final Classes: In Java, a class that is declared with the final keyword cannot be subclassed. This is used to prevent inheritance and to ensure that the class's behavior remains unchanged.
  • String Class: The String class in Java is declared as final. T

m_Item1.QuestionText: If obj instanceof ClassName returns false, what does it mean?

Options.OptionText: obj is not an instance of that class or its subclasses

m_Item1.Explanation: instanceof operator helps in downcasting by checking whether given object is object of the specified classname or its subclass and whether casting is possible.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When a subclass inherits from a superclass, what does it gain?

Options.OptionText: All methods and properties of the superclass (except private ones)
A copy of the superclass constructor

m_Item1.Explanation: Subclass inherits all the data members and member methods except private ones. Constructors are not inherited
